name: AI & Data Science — Sem 2
description: Unsupervised learning, deep learning architectures, time series, recommenders, deployment, ethics and advanced topics.

theory: Unsupervised learning extracts patterns from data without explicit labels, revealing clusters, structure, or latent representations.
Common tasks include clustering, density estimation, and dimensionality reduction. These techniques help with data exploration, anomaly detection, and pretraining.
Evaluation is often harder than supervised learning and may rely on domain metrics, silhouette scores, or downstream task performance when labels are unavailable.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data, such as clusters or low-dimensional embeddings, without direct supervision.

keyPoints: Evaluation can be intrinsic or downstream
Useful for anomaly detection and preprocessing

examples.label: Clustering use
examples.content: Segment customers using k-means for targeted marketing.

examples.label: Embedding
examples.content: Use PCA or autoencoders to reduce dimensionality before visualization.

content.notes: Choose evaluation metrics and preprocessing carefully for unsupervised tasks.

examQuestions.question: Define unsupervised learning and give one example.

theory: K-means partitions data into clusters by minimizing within-cluster variance but requires k and assumes spherical clusters.
DBSCAN finds clusters of arbitrary shape by density and marks noise points; parameters eps and minPts control sensitivity.
Hierarchical clustering builds dendrograms to inspect nested cluster structure and is useful when the number of clusters is unknown.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Clustering groups similar data points without labels; algorithms differ by objective and assumptions.

examples.label: K-means example
examples.content: Run k-means with k=3 and inspect centroids.

examples.label: DBSCAN example
examples.content: Detect outliers as points not in dense regions.

content.notes: Use elbow or silhouette to pick k; visualize clusters after dimensionality reduction.

theory: Dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data into fewer dimensions while preserving structure. PCA preserves variance; t-SNE and UMAP focus on neighborhood structure.
t-SNE excels at visualizing local clusters but can distort global relationships; UMAP often preserves more global structure and is faster on large datasets.
Use reduction for visualization, denoising, or as preprocessing for models; always standardize before PCA and interpret nonlinear embeddings cautiously.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Dimensionality reduction maps high-dimensional data to a lower-dimensional space for visualization or preprocessing.

formulas: Covariance eigen-decomposition: C = V Λ V^T

examples.label: PCA example
examples.content: Project MNIST to 2D with PCA to inspect class overlap.

examples.label: t-SNE example
examples.content: Visualize embeddings with tuned perplexity.

content.notes: Nonlinear embeddings are for visualization; avoid overinterpreting distances.

theory: Architectural choices match data modalities: MLPs for tabular, CNNs for images, RNNs for sequences, and Transformers for attention-based modeling.
Residual connections and normalization improve training for deep nets; pretraining and transfer learning can reduce labeled-data requirements.
Self-attention enables global interaction modeling and scales well with compute when trained on large corpora, enabling state-of-the-art results across modalities.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Neural architectures specify layer types and connections (convolution, recurrence, attention) chosen to match data and tasks.

examples.label: CNN use
examples.content: Apply conv + pooling for image classification.

examples.label: Transformer use
examples.content: Use self-attention for long-range text dependencies.

content.notes: Consider compute and data trade-offs when choosing architectures.
references: Vaswani et al., Attention Is All You Need

theory: CNNs use shared spatial filters to detect local patterns that compose into higher-level features through depth.
Pooling reduces dimensionality and adds translation invariance; modern designs often use strided convolutions and normalization instead of heavy pooling.
Pretrained CNNs can be fine-tuned for specific vision tasks, saving compute and labeled data.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: CNNs apply learned filters across spatial dimensions to build hierarchical image representations.

examples.label: Edge filters
examples.content: First conv layers often learn edge detectors.

examples.label: Transfer learning
examples.content: Fine-tune pretrained ResNet on small dataset.

content.notes: Visualize activations and filters to interpret learned features.

examQuestions.question: Plan transfer learning for small dataset.

theory: RNNs maintain hidden states to process sequences; LSTM/GRU add gating to manage long-range dependencies.
Transformers replace recurrence with self-attention to model global interactions in parallel and rely on positional encodings to capture order.
Transformers scale well with large data and have become dominant in many sequence modeling tasks.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: RNNs use recurrence for sequences; Transformers use attention to capture pairwise interactions across positions.

formulas: h_t = tanh(W_h h_{t−1} + W_x x_t + b)

examples.label: Language modeling
examples.content: Train RNN/Transformer to predict next token probabilities.

examples.label: Time-series
examples.content: Use LSTM for short-term forecasting.

content.notes: Use pretrained checkpoints for practical tasks to reduce compute.

theory: Time series data has temporal dependencies with trend and seasonality components. Stationarity simplifies modeling and forecasting.
ACF and PACF help identify lag structures for AR/MA components; ARIMA models combine autoregressive and moving average terms with differencing.
Modern ML approaches such as sequence models and hybrid decomposition+ML pipelines can capture complex dynamics.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Time series analysis models data indexed by time for forecasting and pattern identification.

formulas: AR(p): x_t = Σ φ_i x_{t−i} + ε_t

examples.label: Sales forecasting
examples.content: Decompose sales and fit ARIMA or ML model for next month.

examples.label: ACF check
examples.content: Use ACF plots to detect significant lags.

content.notes: Beware temporal leakage when building features; use time-aware splits.

examQuestions.question: Design a short forecasting approach for monthly data.

theory: Recommender systems predict user preferences using interaction data and item attributes. Collaborative filtering leverages patterns in user–item matrices; content-based methods use item features.
Matrix factorization uncovers latent factors for users and items; hybrid systems combine signals for robustness and cold-start mitigation.
Evaluation focuses on ranking metrics and online experimentation (A/B testing) for user-facing impact.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Recommendation systems predict and rank items for users based on interactions and features.

keyPoints: Matrix factorization predicts via latent vectors
formulas: R ≈ U V^T, ŷ_ui = u_u^T v_i

examples.label: Collaborative filtering
examples.content: Use SVD on ratings matrix to predict missing entries.

examples.label: Content-based
examples.content: Recommend articles with similar TF-IDF vectors.

content.notes: Address sparsity with regularization and implicit feedback techniques.

examQuestions.question: Design a recommender for a small catalog.

theory: Deploying models requires attention to latency, scalability, and reliability. Packaging models, versioning, and testing are key for production readiness.
MLOps extends DevOps with pipelines for reproducible training, model registries, and monitoring for drift and performance degradation.
Monitoring inputs and model outputs, automating retraining triggers, and governance processes reduce risk in production ML systems.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: MLOps combines ML and DevOps practices to deploy and maintain models reliably at scale.

keyPoints: Monitor for data drift and performance

examples.label: Serving example
examples.content: Wrap model as REST API with FastAPI and containerize.

examples.label: Monitoring
examples.content: Track distribution shifts and alert on drift.

content.notes: Store metadata, seeds, and environments for reproducibility.

theory: AI systems can reproduce or amplify societal biases in training data leading to unfair outcomes; detecting and mitigating bias is essential.
Privacy-preserving techniques such as differential privacy and secure computation limit leakage of individual data points from models and outputs.
Governance, documentation, and stakeholder engagement are necessary complements to technical mitigations to deploy responsible AI.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Ethics in AI covers fairness, privacy, accountability and societal impacts of models and data-driven systems.

keyPoints: Bias can arise from data and modeling choices

examples.label: Bias example
examples.content: A hiring model trained on biased past hires may unfairly penalize certain groups.

examples.label: Privacy example
examples.content: Add DP noise when releasing statistics to avoid re-identification.

content.notes: Pair technical and policy approaches for real-world mitigation.

examQuestions.question: Propose steps for a bias audit.

pyqs.question: What does small ε imply in DP?

references: Fairness and Machine Learning — Barocas et al.

theory: Explainability methods provide local and global views of model behavior; local methods like LIME and SHAP attribute contributions to individual predictions.
Global analyses summarize feature importance and partial dependence to understand model tendencies across datasets.
Interpretability supports debugging, trust, and regulatory compliance but must be validated with domain expertise.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Explainability comprises methods that make model decisions understandable to humans at local or global scales.

keyPoints: Explainability complements but does not replace governance

examples.label: SHAP example
examples.content: Use SHAP to show which features increased predicted risk for a patient.

examples.label: PDP
examples.content: Plot model response as one feature varies.

content.notes: Check stability of explanations across similar inputs.

theory: Reinforcement learning trains agents to maximize cumulative reward via policies and value functions, balancing exploration and exploitation.
Generative models such as GANs, VAEs, and diffusion models learn to synthesize realistic data; training stability and evaluation are active research areas.
Both topics require careful problem formulation, reward shaping (RL), and evaluation metrics; start with simple environments and models.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: RL optimizes policies from reward; generative models learn to produce synthetic data resembling real distributions.

examples.label: RL gridworld
examples.content: Train agent to reach goal while maximizing reward.

examples.label: GAN example
examples.content: Train generator and discriminator adversarially to produce images.

content.notes: Advanced topics may need significant compute; focus on core principles first.
